title: Data & Storage
description: Room database, DataStore, auto-migrations, indexing, WAL mode, and offline-first sync
lesson:
Local Data Persistence

Android offers several storage options, each suited for different use cases:

  • Room — SQLite abstraction for structured data with compile-time query verification
  • DataStore — Key-value or typed data storage, replacing SharedPreferences
  • File storage — For large files (images, downloads)

Room Architecture

@Entity → defines a table @Dao → defines queries (interface) @Database → ties it together (abstract class)

Room validates SQL at compile time, supports reactive queries with Flow, and handles migrations for schema changes.

Room Auto-Migrations

Room 2.4+ can auto-generate migrations for simple schema changes (add column, add table, add index):

@Database( version = 3, entities = [User::class, Post::class], autoMigrations = [ AutoMigration(from = 1, to = 2), AutoMigration(from = 2, to = 3, spec = Migration2To3::class) ] ) abstract class AppDatabase : RoomDatabase() { ... } // Spec needed for ambiguous changes (rename, delete) @RenameColumn(tableName = "users", fromColumnName = "name", toColumnName = "full_name") class Migration2To3 : AutoMigrationSpec
When auto-migration works: Adding columns, tables, indices. When you need manual migration: Complex data transforms, renaming columns (needs spec), changing column types.

Room Indexing

@Entity( indices = [ Index("email", unique = true), Index("lastName", "firstName") ] ) data class User( @PrimaryKey val id: Long, val email: String, val firstName: String, val lastName: String )

Indices speed up WHERE and ORDER BY queries but slow down INSERT/UPDATE (index must be maintained). Add them for columns you query frequently.

WAL Mode

Room uses Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) by default. This allows concurrent reads and writes — readers don't block writers and vice versa. WAL writes changes to a log file first, then merges into the main database during checkpoints.

Offline-First Sync

The offline-first pattern: write to local DB first, sync to server in background:

// Repository pattern for offline-first class ArticleRepository( private val localDao: ArticleDao, private val remoteApi: ArticleApi ) { fun getArticles(): Flow<List<Article>> = localDao.observeAll() suspend fun refresh() { val remote = remoteApi.fetchAll() localDao.upsertAll(remote) // Local DB is source of truth } }
Conflict resolution: When local and remote both change the same record, common strategies are: last-write-wins (simple, may lose data), server-wins (safe default), or merge (complex but preserves both changes).

questions.title: Room DAO
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to define a Room DAO.
questions.explanation: @Dao marks the interface. @Query allows custom SQL with :paramName binding. @Insert and @Delete handle common operations. onConflict = REPLACE creates an upsert.
hints: Room uses annotations to generate SQL boilerplate

questions.title: Room on Main Thread
questions.description: This code crashes: "Cannot access database on the main thread." Fix it.

checks.message: Use Dispatchers.IO or collect a Flow for database operations

questions.explanation: Room throws if you access the database on the main thread. Use a Flow return type in the DAO — Room runs the query on a background thread and emits updates reactively. collectAsState() converts it to Compose state.
hints: Room can return Flow<List<T>> for reactive queries

questions.title: DataStore vs SharedPreferences
questions.description: Why does Google recommend DataStore over SharedPreferences?
options: DataStore is faster for large datasets
DataStore is async, type-safe (Proto), handles errors, and does not block the UI thread
DataStore encrypts data by default
DataStore supports SQL queries
questions.explanation: SharedPreferences blocks the calling thread, can fail silently, and has no type safety. DataStore is fully async via Coroutines/Flow, has proper error handling, and Proto DataStore adds type safety with Protocol Buffers.
hints: What happens when you call SharedPreferences.getString() on the main thread?

questions.title: Room Migration
questions.description: What does this Room migration do?
options: Renames the users table to version 3
Adds an email column and index when upgrading from DB version 2 to 3
Creates a completely new users table
Backs up the database
questions.explanation: Migration(2, 3) runs when upgrading from version 2 to 3. It adds a non-null email column with a default value (so existing rows are valid) and creates an index for faster queries. Without migrations, Room would drop and recreate the database, losing all data.
hints: The two numbers represent source and target versions

questions.title: Preferences DataStore
questions.description: Write a DataStore-backed class that stores and retrieves a user's preferred theme (dark/light) as a string.

checks.message: Expose a Flow for reactive reading
Use suspend edit function for writing

questions.explanation: preferencesDataStore creates a DataStore delegate. stringPreferencesKey creates a typed key. Reading returns a Flow that emits on changes. Writing uses edit { } which is a suspend function handling transactions atomically.
hints: DataStore uses typed keys: stringPreferencesKey, intPreferencesKey, etc.
Reading is a Flow, writing is a suspend function

questions.title: Room Relationship
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to define a one-to-many relationship between User and their Posts in Room.

blanks.hint: Includes all columns of the nested entity

questions.explanation: @PrimaryKey marks primary key fields. @Embedded includes the parent entity's columns. @Relation defines how child entities relate via parentColumn and entityColumn. Room automatically joins the tables when querying UserWithPosts.
hints: Room uses annotations to define relationships between entities
The relationship connects a parent column to a child column

questions.title: Room Auto-Migration
questions.description: What does this auto-migration configuration do?
options: It creates the database from scratch with version 3
It defines two auto-migrations: v1→v2 is fully automatic, v2→v3 renames the 'name' column to 'full_name' using a migration spec
It deletes all data when upgrading from v1 to v3
It only works on new installations, not upgrades
questions.explanation: Room auto-migrations handle simple schema changes automatically. The v1→v2 migration needs no spec (probably just adds a column or table). The v2→v3 migration uses @RenameColumn in a spec class because Room can't guess that "name" became "full_name" (it looks like a delete + add). Without the spec, Room would drop the old column and add a new empty one, losing data.
hints: Renames are ambiguous — Room sees a column disappear and a new one appear
The spec resolves ambiguity by telling Room it's the same column with a new name

questions.title: Room Indexing
questions.description: When should you add an @Index to a Room entity column?
options: Always — indices make everything faster
When you frequently query or sort by that column — indices speed up reads but slow down writes because the index must be maintained
Only for primary key columns
Never — Room automatically indexes everything
questions.explanation: Indices are a read/write tradeoff. They create a sorted data structure that speeds up WHERE clauses and ORDER BY on the indexed column(s). But every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must also update the index, slowing writes. Add indices for columns used in: frequent queries, JOIN conditions, foreign keys, and ORDER BY. Don't index columns that are rarely queried or on tables with heavy write traffic.
hints: Indices are like a book's index — fast lookups, but costs space and maintenance

questions.title: WAL Mode
questions.description: What advantage does WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) provide in Room, and why is it the default?
options: WAL encrypts all database writes for security
WAL allows concurrent reads and writes — readers don't block writers, improving performance in multi-threaded apps
WAL compresses the database file to save storage
WAL prevents data loss by writing to two databases simultaneously
questions.explanation: WAL mode writes changes to a separate log file instead of modifying the database directly. This means readers see a consistent snapshot while writers append to the log — no locking conflicts. Without WAL (journal mode), writes lock the entire database, blocking all readers. WAL is ideal for Android where coroutines may read and write concurrently. Checkpoints periodically merge the WAL file into the main database.
hints: Think about what happens when one coroutine reads while another writes

questions.title: Offline-First Repository
questions.description: Write a repository that follows the offline-first pattern: always read from the local database, sync from network in the background, and expose data as a Flow.

checks.message: Return a Flow for reactive updates
Save remote data to local DB

questions.explanation: The offline-first pattern: (1) UI observes the local database via Flow — always has data to show, even offline. (2) refresh() fetches from the network and upserts into the local DB. (3) Because the UI observes the DB, it automatically updates when new data is inserted. The local DB is the single source of truth — the UI never reads from the network directly. This pattern is recommended by Google's architecture guide.
hints: The UI should never read from the network directly
Room Flow queries automatically emit when data changes
